app.title: Pad & Fan Greenhouse Cooling Calculator
app.subtitle: Free engineering tool for MENA greenhouse cooling design

step1.title: Greenhouse Configuration
step1.description: Define the physical structure of your greenhouse
step1.widthTooltip: The total width of the greenhouse structure measured from wall to wall
step1.lengthTooltip: The total length of the greenhouse structure
step1.ridgeHeightTooltip: The highest point of the greenhouse roof from ground level
step1.eaveHeightTooltip: The height where the roof meets the side wall — the minimum clear height inside
step1.canopyHeightTooltip: The expected maximum height of the crop canopy. Determines the volume where cooling must be effective
step1.padWallTooltip: Which wall the cooling pads are installed on. This defines the direction of airflow through the greenhouse
step1.padHeightTooltip: The vertical height of the installed cooling pads. Cannot exceed the eave height
step1.padThicknessTooltip: Thicker pads provide higher cooling efficiency. 10 cm is standard for most MENA greenhouses; 15 cm recommended above 45°C
step1.coverMaterialTooltip: The covering material affects how much solar heat enters the greenhouse. Single poly lets in the most heat; polycarbonate the least
step1.shadeScreenTooltip: Shade screens reduce solar heat load. External screens are more effective but more expensive
step1.shadePercentTooltip: The percentage of solar radiation blocked by the shade screen (typically 30–70%)
step1.orientationTooltip: N-S orientation distributes solar radiation more evenly; E-W orientation may increase south-wall heat gain in MENA
step1.ventTypeTooltip: Existing vents reduce static pressure and improve fan efficiency when open during cooling
step1.ventAreaTooltip: Total open area of all vents combined

step2.title: Climate & Crop Inputs
step2.description: Define outdoor conditions and your target crop
step2.locationTooltip: Select your location to auto-fill weather data. All values can be manually adjusted
step2.peakSummerTooltip: Override with the hottest recorded conditions from last summer for this location — recommended for system design
step2.externalTempTooltip: The outdoor dry-bulb temperature. This is the starting point for all cooling calculations
step2.externalRHTooltip: Outdoor relative humidity. Lower humidity = more evaporative cooling potential. MENA summers are typically 5–20%
step2.solarRadiationTooltip: Incoming solar radiation intensity. Higher values increase heat gain inside the greenhouse
step2.designScenarioTooltip: Design for the worst conditions your system will face. Peak summer is recommended for MENA
step2.cropTooltip: Select your crop to evaluate whether the cooling system can maintain suitable growing temperatures
step2.growthStageTooltip: Some crops have tighter temperature tolerances during flowering and fruiting stages

results.title: Your Results

preview.title: Estimated Indoor Temperature

emailGate.title: Unlock Your Full Report
emailGate.description: Get your complete greenhouse cooling analysis — including equipment sizing, crop suitability assessment, and temperature gradient map.
emailGate.name: Your Name
emailGate.privacy: We respect your privacy. Your data is used only to deliver your report.

crop.title: Crop Suitability
crop.achievable: Achievable — indoor temperature is within the optimal range for {{crop}}
crop.marginal: Marginal — indoor temperature is close to the stress threshold for {{crop}}. Consider additional cooling measures
crop.notAchievable: Not Achievable — indoor temperature exceeds the stress threshold for {{crop}}. System redesign recommended

system.title: System Sizing
system.padVelocityLow: Below optimal — system may be oversized for pad area
system.padVelocityHigh: Above optimal — risk of water carry-over. Increase pad area

actions.ctaDescription: Get expert guidance on your greenhouse cooling system design

results.disclaimer: These results are engineering estimates based on the Stull (2011) wet-bulb approximation and ASABE airflow standards. Actual performance may vary based on site-specific conditions, installation quality, and maintenance. A licensed professional should verify final design decisions.

validation.eaveExceedsRidge: Eave height cannot exceed ridge height
validation.canopyExceedsEave: Canopy height cannot exceed eave height
validation.padExceedsEave: Pad height cannot exceed eave height
validation.padThicknessRange: Pad thickness must be between 1 and 30 cm
validation.solarRange: Solar radiation must be between 0 and 1400 W/m²
validation.invalidEmail: Please enter a valid email address

education.title: Understanding Pad & Fan Greenhouse Cooling
education.section1Title: How Pad & Fan Evaporative Cooling Works
education.section1: Pad and fan cooling is the most widely used active cooling method for greenhouses in hot, arid climates. The system draws outside air through wet cellulose pads installed on one wall of the greenhouse. As the air passes through the water-saturated pads, evaporation absorbs heat energy from the air, reducing its temperature. Large exhaust fans on the opposite wall create negative pressure that pulls this cooled air across the entire greenhouse length before exhausting it outside. The temperature of the air entering the greenhouse is determined by the evaporative cooling process — it can never go below the wet bulb temperature of the outside air, which is the theoretical cooling limit.
education.section2Title: Why Wet Bulb Temperature is the True Cooling Limit
education.section2: In the MENA region, summers bring extreme dry-bulb temperatures of 45–50°C, but relative humidity often drops to 5–15%. This combination creates a large wet bulb depression — the gap between dry bulb and wet bulb temperature — which is the true measure of evaporative cooling potential. For example, at 48°C and 10% relative humidity in Riyadh, the wet bulb temperature is approximately 22°C, giving a wet bulb depression of 26°C. With a high-efficiency 15cm pad (92% saturation effectiveness), the achievable temperature drop is about 24°C, bringing air entering the greenhouse down to roughly 24°C at the pad face. This is why pad and fan systems are particularly effective in the dry MENA climate — the low humidity is actually an advantage for evaporative cooling.
education.section3Title: Common Sizing Mistakes in MENA Greenhouses
education.section3: The most frequent mistake is undersizing the system based on average conditions rather than designing for peak summer. A system that works well in April will fail in July if not designed for worst-case conditions. Other common errors include: using 5cm pads in regions exceeding 42°C external temperature, making the airflow path too long without accounting for the temperature gradient (temperature rises along the greenhouse length as cooled air absorbs solar heat), ignoring the effect of cover material and shade on solar heat gain, and failing to maintain pads and water distribution systems — dry patches on pads dramatically reduce cooling efficiency.
education.section4Title: When Pad & Fan Alone is Not Enough
education.section4: Pad and fan cooling has physical limits. When outdoor humidity is high (above 60%), the wet bulb depression shrinks and evaporative cooling becomes ineffective. In coastal MENA locations, or during humid periods, additional strategies may be needed: fogging systems inside the greenhouse for supplemental cooling, shade screens (40–70% shade) to reduce solar heat gain before it enters the air stream, hybrid systems combining pad and fan with mechanical refrigeration for the most sensitive crops, and night ventilation strategies to pre-cool the greenhouse structure before the next day's heat peak. The calculator above helps you determine whether pad and fan alone will meet your crop's temperature requirements, or whether supplemental cooling is needed.

footer.disclaimer: This tool provides engineering estimates only. Results should be verified by a qualified professional.
